subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Elected Legislature vs. Independent Judiciary in Preserving Limited Government
stimulus: There is continued debate over how to best preserve the democratic ideal of limited government. Develop an argument as to whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Federalist No. 51, Federalist No. 78, Article I of the Constitution of the United States.)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents in the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: An elected legislature is more effective at preserving limited government because they are elected by the majority, held accountable through elections, and can check executive overreach through appropriations and oversight.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The independent judiciary is more effective at preserving limited government because they are not accountable to the wishes of the majority and can strike down unconstitutional laws regardless of political pressure.

wrong_examples: There are debates about whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government. (Describes a debate — does not take a position)
The executive branch best protects limited government. (Responds to the wrong prompt — the executive branch is not one of the two options in the prompt)
common_traps: Must choose between the ELECTED LEGISLATURE and the INDEPENDENT JUDICIARY — a thesis about the executive branch or a third institution does not respond to the prompt
Limited government means constraining governmental power to protect individual rights — the thesis should connect the chosen institution to this specific democratic ideal

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award the HIGHEST tier met. Tier 2 and 3 require Row A. Tier 3 requires at least one piece from Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions Federalist 51, Federalist 78, Article I, judicial review, Marbury v. Madison, checks and balances, the Bill of Rights, separation of powers, or another relevant concept about limited government.

wrong_examples: Completely off-topic evidence with no connection to limited government, legislatures, or the judiciary
common_traps: Tier 1 does NOT require Row A and evidence only needs to be topically relevant
Judicial review (established in Marbury v. Madison) is a key concept for this prompt — it is how the independent judiciary strikes down unconstitutional laws

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. Requires Row A.

required_elements: One specific piece that directly supports the thesis, OR two relevant pieces
alternatives.correct_example: Cites Federalist 78's argument that the judiciary, being the 'least dangerous branch' because it controls neither the sword nor the purse, can protect constitutional limits without threatening liberty — directly supporting the thesis that an independent judiciary preserves limited government.

wrong_examples: Cites Federalist 78 without explaining Hamilton's argument about judicial independence and how it connects to preserving limited government
common_traps: Federalist 78 (Hamilton) argues for judicial independence and judicial review as the means by which the courts protect the Constitution against legislative overreach — it strongly supports the independent judiciary thesis

Federalist 51 (Madison) argues for checks and balances among all branches — it can support the legislature thesis by showing that Congress's power to check the executive preserves limited government; Tier 2 REQUIRES Row A

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, at least one from a listed foundational document (Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I). Requires Row A.

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis
At least one from: Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Federalist 78's defense of judicial independence and judicial review to show how an unelected court can nullify unconstitutional laws, AND cites Marbury v. Madison as the historical precedent establishing judicial review — both supporting the thesis that an independent judiciary is more effective at preserving limited government.

wrong_examples: Two pieces of evidence but neither from Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I
Uses Federalist 51 to argue the judiciary should have unlimited power (misreads Federalist 51, which argues for BALANCED power among ALL branches, not judicial supremacy)
common_traps: Article I establishes the legislative branch and its enumerated powers — for the legislature thesis, Article I shows the scope of congressional authority to limit executive power and pass constitutional laws

Federalist 51's famous quote 'ambition must be made to counteract ambition' supports checks and balances — it can be used to argue either branch preserves limited government through its ability to check the other

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain the logical connection between evidence and the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Article I establishes the legislative branch and sets qualifications and powers for members of Congress. Article I preserves limited government because it allows voters to respond to decisions made by the legislature through regular elections, creating an ongoing check on legislative overreach.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Federalist No. 78 explains that the judicial branch is not elected and holds lifetime appointments. The judicial branch protects limited government because it is insulated from political pressures that might lead elected officials to expand government power to satisfy popular demands — an elected legislature might grow government to win votes, but judges have no such incentive.

wrong_examples: Federalist 78 says the courts protect the Constitution. (Restates the evidence without explaining the causal link to limited government specifically)
The legislature passes laws, which is important. (Does not connect to the limited government thesis)
common_traps: The reasoning must connect to LIMITED GOVERNMENT specifically — explain how the institution's structure or powers prevent governmental overreach
Requires Row A AND Row B ≥ 2 pts

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it — not merely acknowledge it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Some people might say that an independent judiciary would best preserve limited government because they are not influenced by public opinion. However, an elected legislature better protects limited government because elections hold legislators directly accountable to the people, making it harder for government to grow without the public's consent.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: It can be argued that an elected legislature is more likely to preserve limited government because they are elected by their constituents. However, since elected officials seek popular approval, they may expand government programs to win votes — the independent judiciary, precisely because it is not elected, is better positioned to resist such expansions of government power.

wrong_examples: An elected legislature is also a strong way to protect limited government. (Does not describe an alternate perspective or engage with a counterargument)

Some may argue the executive branch is the best protector of limited government, which I would agree. (The executive branch is not one of the two options in the prompt — does not constitute a relevant alternate perspective)
common_traps: The alternate perspective must be the OTHER option in the prompt (if arguing for elected legislature, the alternate is independent judiciary and vice versa)
Requires Row A — without a thesis, Row D cannot be earned
